articles.snippet: Kitco NEWS has a diverse team of journalists reporting on the economy, stock markets, commodities, cryptocurrencies, mining and metals with accuracy and objectivity. Jeremy Szafron joins Kitco News as an anchor and producer from Kitco’s Vancouver bureau. Jeremy began his career in 2006 as a Journalist at CTV (Canada’s largest network), initially engaging audiences as an entertainment reporter before pivoting to business reporting focusing on mining and small-caps. Building on this experience, Jeremy developed an online video news program for PressReader, launching them into a new direction. Following this success, he launched Investor Scene and Initiate Research, news platforms providing exclusive market insights and deal-flow opportunities in mining and Canadian small-caps. Jeremy has also worked as a market strategist and investor relations consultant with various publicly traded companies in the mining, energy, CPG, and tech industries. Now, as an Anchor at Kitco News, Jeremy will continue to inform a global audience of the latest developments and critical themes in finance and commodities.

Mars is one of the most successful songwriters in the history of the music business, with 10 No. 1s on Billboard’s Hot 100 including one earlier this year for his single “I Just Might” from his latest album *The Romantic* and another last year with global smash duet “Die With A Smile” with Lady Gaga. “We are thrilled to welcome Bruno to Avex and look forward to supporting his incredible songs,” Avex Music Group CEO Brandon Silverstein said on Tuesday.

articles.title: What Zohran Mamdani's favorite rappers shows about his politics - USA Today
articles.snippet: ## Mamdani chooses uber-capitalists Biggie, Nas and Jay-Z among his five favorite rappers. While New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani may not agree with the Democratic Party’s centrist wing on much, he does have mainstream taste in hip hop. And the new mayor's choices were almost as unsurprising: four widely revered legends who rose to fame in the 1990s − Nas, Common, Jay-Z, and the Notorious B.I.G., better known as Biggie − and one almost as prominent from the 2000s, Lupe Fiasco. For example, the moderate House Democratic Leader Rep. Hakeem Jeffries put three of the same rappers − Biggie, Jay-Z and Nas − in his top own top five when asked for his list in 2020. It's not a coincidence that Mamdani and Jeffries both chose Nas, Biggie and Jay-Z. Admiring Common is not the only thing Mamdani has in common with the former president, who also famously enjoyed listening to Jay-Z — or maybe Mamdani was never really as edgy and radical as both his supporters and opponents imagined in the first place.
articles.summary: What Zohran Mamdani's favorite rappers shows about his politics - USA Today

articles.title: Money matters in practice - Nature
articles.snippet: **Rhiannon Jones** spoke with Jay Dhaliwal, an independent chartered financial planner, about how dental hygienists and dental therapists can build financial confidence and protect themselves both professionally and personally. In the most recent British Society of Dental Hygiene and Therapy (BSDHT) *Dental Health Matters* podcast, Rhiannon Jones and Jay Dhaliwal explored why understanding protection, planning and personal finances matters throughout a clinical career. Jay clarified that professional protection on its own does not address the realities of how many dental hygienists and dental therapists work. For those who are self-employed, work across several practices or rely on limited employer benefits (such as the NHS), personal financial planning often determines how resilient a career really is when circumstances change. Rather than presenting financial planning as a structured checklist, Jay framed it as a way for clinicians to make informed choices in careers that are rarely linear. Jay Dhaliwal is an independent chartered financial planner at Chase De Vere with 10 years' experience supporting dental professionals.
articles.summary: Money matters in practice - Nature

articles.title: Jay-Z Is Playing Yankee Stadium This Summer: Who Are the Biggest Acts to Ever Play There? - Billboard
articles.snippet: # Jay-Z Is Playing Yankee Stadium This Summer: Who Are the Biggest Acts to Ever Play There? In advance of his two July dates, *Billboard* looks at the biggest Yankee Stadium shows in Boxscore history. Jay-Z announced on Wednesday (March 18) his first two headline concerts in seven years with shows at Yankee Stadium on July 10-11. ### Jay-Z Announces Two Yankee Stadium Shows Celebrating ‘Reasonable Doubt’ & ‘The Blueprint’ Anniversaries. Jay-Z “made the Yankee hat more famous than a Yankee can,” as he famously rapped in 2009’s “Empire State of Mind,” but it’s been even longer since he played at the iconic stadium. Before that, he did a hometown swap with Eminem, playing two shows at Detroit’s Comerica Park and two at Yankee Stadium in 2010. The earliest Yankee Stadium concert reported to Billboard Boxscore featured Tracy Chapman headlining a rally to celebrate Nelson Mandela’s release from prison in 1990. Keep reading for the five biggest Boxscore reports from Yankee Stadium, by the numbers.
articles.summary: Jay-Z Is Playing Yankee Stadium This Summer: Who Are the Biggest Acts to Ever Play There? - Billboard

Jackie Chan and director Leo Zhang have teamed for “Pawfect Agents,” an action comedy set to begin production in September, with international sales company Cappu Films handling pre-sales and introducing the project to buyers at Hong Kong FilMart. The live-action and CG hybrid feature is directed and produced by Zhang, whose previous credits include “Fox Hunt” and “Bleeding Steel.” LinKEY Sanxing Film and Zhang’s Shangjia Film are key partners in the project’s development and production. Unlike many of his classic films, where he teams up with human partners, this time his allies are a group of animal agents brought to life through a blend of live-action and CG,” Zhang said. “The project represents a rare cultural and technological exchange between some of Europe’s most admired heritage sites and a Chinese film production, allowing the film to capture the grandeur of iconic scenery while introducing a lively new adventure that we believe will resonate strongly with audiences worldwide.”.

Jay-Z is set to celebrate the anniversaries of his seminal albums *Reasonable Doubt* and *The Blueprint* with a pair of concerts at Yankee Stadium in New York this summer, the rapper’s company Roc Nation confirmed on Wednesday. The first show celebrates the 30th anniversary of Jay’s debut album *Reasonable Doubt* and will take place on Friday, July 10, and the second show is set for the following day, July 11, and commemorates the 25th anniversary of *The Blueprint.* Both records are considered among the greatest rap albums of all time, earning platinum certifications from the RIAA. The news comes a day after Jay-Z was confirmed to headline the upcoming Roots Picnic in Philadelphia in May. Public performances from Jay-Z have become rarer in recent years, and he hasn’t had a full solo tour since his 4:44 Tour back in 2017.

articles.title: Meaningful reason behind Jay-Z’s name change revealed - Page Six
articles.snippet: # Meaningful reason behind Jay-Z’s name change revealed. **Jay-Z** aficionados noticed this week that the hip hop icon has added an umlaut to his name. An announcement that the “99 Problems” rapper is joining The Roots for the band’s two-day Philly music fest was billed as, “JAŸ-Z and The Roots,” and his artist name on Spotify, YouTube and more is now spelled the same way. Pals of the star pointed out to us that Jay used the same German punctuation on the cover art of his 1996 release, “Reasonable Doubt.” And this year marks the album’s 30th anniversary. Jay-Z is partial to punctuation: In 2013, he dropped the hyphen from his name, then brought it back in 2017, changing it to all caps. He reformatted his moniker as JAY:Z for his “4:44” album, making him a copy editor’s nightmare. Either way, “JAŸ-Z and The Roots” will headline a May 30 gig for the Roots Picnic.

articles.title: Jay-Z Defends Billionaires: ‘Morality Is Not Defined By A Dollar Amount’ - Forbes
articles.snippet: Jay-Z, the world’s wealthiest musician, pushed back on arguments that billionaires are inherently bad in a rare interview with GQ on Tuesday, saying it’s “like a cop-out” to link a person’s morality to the amount of money they have. Jay-Z, whom Forbes estimates is worth $2.8 billion, told GQ a person’s “morality is not defined by a dollar amount,” questioning: “If so, what is that dollar amount? When asked how he feels about the “public perception that all billionaires are bad,” Jay-Z said “people behave the way they want to behave—it’s not a dollar amount” and criticized the argument as a “cop-out.”. Jay-Z said critics of billionaires “demonize this group of folks without fixing the actual system that exists,” adding, “[Money] may enhance it or may cause you to act in a way. I don’t like that,” Jay-Z said, saying it was “like trying to tear down people’s lives.” He said his company chose Lamar to headline the Super Bowl because he was having a “monster year,” not to make a statement on the rap feud.
